title: Grouped-Query Attention
description: An attention variant that reduces KV cache memory by sharing key-value heads across query groups.
problemStatement: KV caches grow with context length and can dominate inference memory for long sequences.
coreIdea: Grouped-query attention (GQA) lets several query heads share fewer key-value heads than standard multi-head attention.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Grouped-query attention is an attention variant derived from multi-head attention. It appears in decoder stacks where models need long-context inference without proportional KV-cache growth.

whatItOptimizes.title: What It Optimizes
whatItOptimizes.body: GQA targets KV-cache size, memory bandwidth during autoregressive decoding, and long-context inference cost while keeping most of the expressiveness of multi-head attention.

practicalBenefit.title: Practical Benefit
practicalBenefit.body: Engineers get lower KV-cache memory and reduced bandwidth per token during inference, which matters most at long context lengths and large batch sizes.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: Queries still use many heads, but keys and values are grouped so multiple query heads read from the same KV head. The compute flow diagram shows how queries route to shared KV groups.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math and Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: With H query heads and G KV groups, each KV head serves H/G query heads. Tensor layouts follow grouped head dimensions instead of one KV pair per query head.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared to Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: GQA sits between multi-head attention (one KV head per query head) and multi-query attention (one KV head for all queries). The comparison table summarizes tradeoffs against MHA and MQA.

variantsAndNearbyModules.title: Variants and Nearby Modules
variantsAndNearbyModules.body: Nearby attention variants include multi-query attention, multi-head attention, and sliding-window or sparse attention patterns that also target memory or compute.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: Many recent open-weight LLMs adopt GQA or MQA-style KV sharing in their decoder blocks. The model list below comes from registry usage fields when populated.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations and Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: Sharing KV heads can slightly reduce representational flexibility versus full MHA. GQA does not replace other optimizations such as quantization, paging, or sparse attention by itself.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: Long-context serving and edge deployment still reward KV-cache savings. GQA remains a practical default when teams want a balance between MHA quality and MQA memory savings.

readerShortcut.title: Reader Shortcut
readerShortcut.body: Think of GQA as sharing KV heads across query groups: fewer KV tensors to store, with most query-head diversity preserved.
